note_data.title: Hydrocarbons
keyPoints: Transition elements are found in the d-block and form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
The d subshell can hold up to 10 electrons
Scandium and zinc are in the d-block but are not transition elements because their ions do not have incomplete d subshells
Scandium forms Sc³⁺ with an empty d subshell, zinc forms Zn²⁺ with a full d subshell
The 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital in atoms
When forming ions, 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons
Transition elements can form multiple oxidation states due to their d orbital configurations

definitions.definition: Elements that form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
A subshell that can hold up to 10 electrons, found in transition elements

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give conditions and observations. Use 'when...then' format.

modelAnswer.question: Explain why alkenes are more reactive than alkanes. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Alkenes contain a C=C double bond which makes them unsaturated. The C=C double bond has a high electron density which attracts electrophiles. Alkenes can undergo addition reactions whereas alkanes are saturated and can only undergo substitution reactions under harsh conditions.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Alkenes contain C=C double bond/are unsaturated
Mark 2: High electron density attracts electrophiles
Mark 3: Alkenes undergo addition reactions/alkanes need harsh conditions

commonMistakes: Thinking scandium and zinc are transition elements because they are in the d-block
Forgetting that 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions
Confusing the filling order (4s before 3d) with the ionization order (4s removed first)
examinerTips: Remember that being in the d-block does not automatically make an element a transition element
Always check for incomplete d orbitals in ions to identify true transition elements
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Free radical substitution mechanism showing initiation (Cl2 → 2Cl•), propagation (CH4 + Cl• → CH3• + HCl, then CH3• + Cl2 → CH3Cl + Cl•) and termination steps
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to distinguish between alkanes and alkenes, including molecular formulas and a chemical test
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying the correct molecular formula for butene
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — students had to explain why ethene decolorizes bromine water while ethane does not
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — required drawing structural formulas for first three alkanes and alkenes, plus naming them

workedExample.question: Propene reacts with bromine water. Write a balanced chemical equation for this reaction and explain what you would observe. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that propene (C₃H₆) is an alkene with a C=C double bond
Step 2: Recognize this is an addition reaction where Br₂ adds across the double bond
Step 3: Write the equation: C₃H₆ + Br₂ → C₃H₆Br₂
Step 4: State the observation: orange/brown bromine water turns colorless
workedExample.answer: C₃H₆ + Br₂ → C₃H₆Br₂. The orange/brown bromine water would turn colorless as the bromine is used up in the addition reaction across the C=C double bond.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct reactants, 1 mark for correct product formula, 1 mark for balanced equation, 1 mark for correct observation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the bromine water test results' — always state that alkenes turn bromine water from orange/brown to colorless, while alkanes show no change
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often wrote incorrect structural formulas' — remember that alkanes have single bonds only (C-C) while alkenes must have at least one double bond (C=C)
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently mixed up molecular formulas' — alkanes follow CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ pattern while alkenes follow CₙH₂ₙ pattern
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw structural formulas showing: 1) Alkane structure (e.g., ethane) with all single C-C and C-H bonds clearly shown, 2) Alkene structure (e.g., ethene) with the C=C double bond prominently displayed, 3) Complete addition reaction diagram showing alkene + bromine → dibromo compound, with all atoms and bonds before and after reaction
note_data.quickRecap: Transition elements are d-block elements that form stable ions with incomplete d orbitals. Scandium and zinc are d-block elements but not transition elements because their ions have complete or empty d subshells. When forming ions, 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons, despite 4s being filled before 3d in neutral atoms.
